Topic: Chapter 20: The DOM — Selecting Modifying and Creating Elements

Description: Establish what the DOM is before introducing any API. The browser does not read HTML as a string after the page loads — it converts it into a tree of live objects called the Document Object Model, where every tag becomes a node and every node is a JavaScript object with properties and methods. Cover document as the entry point to the entire tree. Cover the four primary selection methods: getElementById(id) which returns a single element or null, querySelector(selector) which accepts any valid CSS selector and returns the first matching element or null, querySelectorAll(selector) which returns a static NodeList of all matches, and getElementsByClassName / getElementsByTagName which return live HTMLCollections (note the live vs static distinction). Cover how to traverse the tree using parentElement, children, firstElementChild, lastElementChild, nextElementSibling, and previousElementSibling. Cover document.documentElement for the root html element and document.body for the body. Establish the mental model: the DOM is the live representation of the page — change the DOM and the page changes immediately.
Learning Outcome: Student can explain what the DOM is, select elements using all four selection methods, explain the difference between a live HTMLCollection and a static NodeList, and traverse parent-child-sibling relationships in the tree.
Activity: Selection Lab: given an HTML page with a navbar, three cards, a form, and a footer, write selection code for each of the following without changing the HTML: select the second card by position using querySelectorAll and bracket notation, select all elements with a given class, select the parent of a specific button, select the next sibling of a heading, select the last child of the card container. Log each to the console and verify type using instanceof.
Project: Chatbot DOM Wiring: open the chatbot HTML file built during the CSS module. Using querySelector and querySelectorAll, select and log every major element: the message list, the input field, the send button, the settings panel. Confirm each selection returns the expected element. This file will grow across all three chapters into the fully wired chatbot interface.

Topic: Modifying Elements: Text Attributes Styles and classList
Description: Cover the two properties for reading and writing element content. textContent: reads and sets the raw text of an element and all its descendants — safe, never parsed as HTML, never creates XSS risk. innerHTML: reads and sets the HTML markup inside an element — powerful but dangerous when used with user-supplied content because the browser parses the string as HTML and executes any embedded script. Establish the rule: use textContent for text content, use innerHTML only for trusted static markup. Cover attribute access: element.getAttribute(name) to read any attribute, element.setAttribute(name, value) to set, element.removeAttribute(name) to remove, and element.hasAttribute(name) to check. Cover direct property access for common attributes: element.id, element.href, element.src, element.alt, element.value, element.checked — these are properties, not attributes, and may differ from getAttribute in edge cases. Cover inline style modification using element.style.propertyName in camelCase — note this creates an inline style that overrides the stylesheet. Cover classList as the preferred way to apply CSS changes: classList.add(name), classList.remove(name), classList.toggle(name) which adds if absent or removes if present, classList.contains(name) returning a boolean, and classList.replace(oldName, newName). Establish the pattern: never modify styles directly for behavioral state changes, add and remove classes instead — the CSS defines what each class looks like.
Learning Outcome: Student can safely read and write element content using textContent, explain why innerHTML is dangerous with user input, access and modify attributes using both methods, and apply class-based style changes using classList.
Activity: Modification Lab: build a live character counter for a textarea that updates a counter element using textContent on each input event. Add a button that toggles a dark-mode class on the body using classList.toggle. Build a card component where clicking a heart button toggles a liked class and changes the button text between Like and Liked using textContent. Use getAttribute and setAttribute to dynamically change the href of a link based on a dropdown selection.
Project: Chatbot DOM Wiring: write a renderMessage(sender, text) function that creates a message string and appends it to the message list using textContent (never innerHTML). Add a function toggleTypingIndicator(visible) that adds or removes a typing class on the bot avatar using classList.toggle. Add a setInputDisabled(disabled) function that sets the disabled attribute on the send button and input field during bot response.

Topic: Creating Appending and Removing Elements Dynamically
Description: Cover the three-step pattern for adding new content to the page: create an element with document.createElement(tagName), configure it by setting its textContent, attributes, and classes, and attach it to the DOM with a method that specifies where it goes. Cover the insertion methods: element.append(...nodes) adds one or more nodes or strings to the end of the element's children, element.prepend(...nodes) adds to the beginning, element.before(node) inserts before the element itself (as a sibling), element.after(node) inserts after. Cover element.appendChild(node) as the older single-child-only version of append. Cover element.remove() for removing an element from the DOM entirely, and element.removeChild(childNode) as the older equivalent. Cover element.replaceWith(newNode) for replacement. Cover cloneNode(deep): cloneNode(false) clones the element without its children, cloneNode(true) clones the full subtree. Cover insertAdjacentHTML(position, html) for inserting HTML strings at specific positions without replacing existing content — positions are beforebegin, afterbegin, beforeend, afterend. Establish the document fragment pattern for inserting many elements efficiently: build the full tree in a DocumentFragment and insert it once to minimize browser reflows.
Learning Outcome: Student can create elements with all necessary properties, insert them at any position in the DOM using the full insertion API, remove elements, clone subtrees, and use DocumentFragment for batch insertions.
Activity: Dynamic List Builder: build a tag-input component where typing a word and pressing Enter creates a new tag element (a span with text and a remove button), appends it to a tag container, clears the input, and focuses it again. Each tag's remove button removes only that tag using element.remove(). Use cloneNode to implement a duplicate button that copies an existing tag. Build the entire list using a DocumentFragment when loading an initial set of tags from an array.
Project: Chatbot DOM Wiring: write a createMessageElement(sender, text, timestamp) function that builds a full message DOM node — the outer container div, inner avatar img, bubble div, sender span, text paragraph, and timestamp span — all created with createElement, configured, and assembled before a single append to the message list. This replaces the string-based renderMessage from the previous article. Confirm the output matches the BEM class structure from the CSS module.

Topic: Chapter 21: Events Event Delegation Form Handling and Interactive Components

Description: Cover the event-driven programming model: rather than code running top to bottom once, browser code waits for user actions and responds. Cover addEventListener(eventType, handler): attaches a function that runs every time the named event fires on that element. Cover removeEventListener: requires the same function reference — anonymous functions registered inline cannot be removed. Cover the event object passed automatically to every handler: event.type is the event name, event.target is the element the event actually fired on, event.currentTarget is the element the listener is attached to (same as this inside a regular function handler), event.preventDefault() stops the browser's default behavior (like form submission or link navigation), event.stopPropagation() stops the event from propagating further up the tree. Cover the most commonly used event types: click for pointer interaction, keydown and keyup for keyboard (event.key gives the key name, event.code gives the physical key), input for live text field updates, change for when an input loses focus after modification, submit for form submission, focus and blur for focus state changes, mouseenter and mouseleave for hover without bubbling, mouseover and mouseout with bubbling. Cover the load and DOMContentLoaded events on document and window for knowing when the page is ready.
Learning Outcome: Student can attach and remove event listeners using named function references, access the event object properties, distinguish target from currentTarget, call preventDefault and stopPropagation when appropriate, and choose the correct event type for each interaction.
Activity: Event Explorer: build a keyboard shortcut manager that logs the pressed key and its code to a panel on every keydown. Use event.key to detect Enter, Escape, and arrow keys and log a specific action for each. Build a color preview box that changes its background to the value of a color input in real time on the input event. Use DOMContentLoaded to ensure all DOM setup code runs only after the HTML is fully parsed.
Project: Chatbot DOM Wiring: attach a click listener to the send button that reads the input value, validates it using the validateMessage function from chatbot-logic.js, and calls renderMessage if valid. Attach a keydown listener to the input field that triggers the same action when the Enter key is pressed without Shift held (Shift+Enter should add a newline). Clear and refocus the input after each send.

Description: Establish the bubbling mechanism first: when an event fires on an element it propagates upward through every ancestor until it reaches the document. A click on a button inside a div also triggers click listeners on that div, its parent, and every ancestor. This is not accidental — it is the foundation of event delegation. Cover event delegation: instead of attaching a separate listener to each child element, attach one listener to their common parent. Inside the handler, use event.target to check which specific child was clicked. This is not just a performance optimization — it is the only correct approach for dynamically created elements, because elements added to the DOM after a listener was registered did not exist when the listener ran and therefore have no listeners of their own. Cover event.target.closest(selector): walks up the DOM from the event target looking for the nearest ancestor matching the selector — this is how to safely find the card, list item, or component root when the click landed on an inner child. Cover event.target.matches(selector) for checking if the target itself matches a condition. Cover stopPropagation and why overusing it causes bugs — intercepting propagation breaks any parent listener that was legitimately waiting for the event. Cover capturing phase vs bubbling phase: listeners fire during bubbling by default, setting the third addEventListener argument to true enables capturing.
Learning Outcome: Student can explain event bubbling, implement event delegation on a parent element, use event.target.closest() to find the nearest matching ancestor, and explain why delegation is required for dynamically created elements.
Activity: Delegation Practice: build a comment section where a container holds dynamically added comments, each with a reply button, a delete button, and a like button. Attach exactly one click listener to the container. Use event.target.closest() inside the handler to determine which button was clicked and which comment it belongs to. Add new comments dynamically and verify that the single parent listener handles their buttons without any additional setup.
Project: Chatbot DOM Wiring: replace any per-message event listeners with a single delegated listener on the message list container. Handle message-level interactions (copy text, timestamp hover reveal) via event.target.closest('.message'). This ensures dynamically created messages from the previous article work immediately without re-registering listeners every time a message is added.

Description: Cover the submit event on a form element: fires when the user submits by button click or Enter key inside an input. Always call event.preventDefault() first to stop the browser from reloading the page. Cover accessing form values: input.value for text inputs (always a string), parseInt or parseFloat to convert numeric input values, input.checked for checkboxes and radio buttons, select.value for dropdowns, textarea.value for multi-line text. Cover the FormData API for gathering all field values at once: new FormData(formElement), formData.get(name), formData.getAll(name) for multi-value fields, and iteration using for...of. Cover constraint validation: the browser's built-in validation attributes (required, minlength, maxlength, pattern, type=email, type=number min max) and the validity object exposed on each input — validity.valid, validity.valueMissing, validity.tooShort, validity.patternMismatch. Cover setCustomValidity(message) for custom validation messages and reportValidity() to trigger the browser's native error display. Cover real-time validation by listening to the input event and updating error messages while typing versus submit-time validation. Cover the accessibility implications: error messages should be linked to their input with aria-describedby.
Learning Outcome: Student can handle form submission without page reload, extract all input values correctly for each input type, use the FormData API, implement both constraint validation and custom validation, and provide real-time error feedback.
Activity: Form Validation Lab: build a multi-field registration form (name, email, password with confirm, age) with the following constraints: name required and at least 2 characters, email valid format, password at least 8 characters with one number, confirm must match password, age must be a number between 13 and 120. Show inline error messages per field in real time on the input event. On submit, if all valid, display a success summary of the entered data. Use setCustomValidity for the password match check.
Project: Chatbot DOM Wiring: the chatbot input bar is a form element. Refactor the send logic to use a form submit event rather than a click listener on the button — this automatically handles both button click and Enter key submission and correctly calls preventDefault. Add validation feedback: show a character count approaching the limit and display an error message if the input fails validateMessage.

Description: Establish the problem first: when multiple parts of the UI need to reflect the same data, reading from and writing to the DOM directly leads to bugs — the DOM becomes both the store and the display of data simultaneously, and they fall out of sync. Introduce the state-first pattern: define application state as a plain JavaScript object, write a render function that builds the DOM purely from that state, and update only the state when something changes then call render. The DOM is now a consequence of the state, not a source of truth. Cover the re-render pattern: on each state update, the render function either rebuilds the UI section from scratch or surgically updates only what changed. Cover localStorage for persisting state across page refreshes: localStorage.setItem(key, value) stores strings only, so objects require JSON.stringify before storing and JSON.parse after reading. localStorage.getItem(key) returns null if the key does not exist — always handle this case. localStorage.removeItem(key) deletes one entry, localStorage.clear() deletes all. Cover loading persisted state on DOMContentLoaded: initialize the state object from localStorage if data exists, otherwise use default values. Establish the save-on-update pattern: every time state changes, serialize and save to localStorage.
Learning Outcome: Student can implement the state-first rendering pattern where the DOM is derived from a state object, write a render function that reflects any state change, and persist application state to localStorage using JSON serialization.
Activity: State and Storage: build a Kanban board with three columns (To Do, In Progress, Done). Model the board state as an object with three arrays of task strings. Write a renderBoard() function that clears and rebuilds all three columns from the state object. Add tasks via a form that pushes to the appropriate array and calls renderBoard. Add drag-and-drop between columns that mutates state and re-renders. Persist the entire board state to localStorage so it survives a page refresh.
Project: Chatbot DOM Wiring: model the chatbot session as a state object: {messages: [], inputValue: '', isLoading: false, error: null}. Write a renderChat() function that rebuilds the message list from state.messages using the createMessageElement function. Update state on every user action and call renderChat. Persist the messages array to localStorage so the conversation history survives a page refresh and loads automatically on DOMContentLoaded.

Topic: Chapter 22: Connecting to Real Data — fetch async/await and Error Handling

Description: Establish the problem: all data so far has lived in the JavaScript file itself — hardcoded arrays and objects. Real applications fetch data from servers that store and compute it. Cover what an API is: a URL endpoint that the browser can send a request to and receive structured data back, usually JSON. Explain the client-server model: the browser (client) sends an HTTP request, the server processes it and returns an HTTP response with a status code and a body. Cover HTTP status codes: 200 (OK), 201 (Created), 400 (Bad Request), 401 (Unauthorized), 404 (Not Found), 500 (Server Error). Cover fetch(url): returns a Promise — a JavaScript object representing an operation that has not finished yet. A Promise is in one of three states: pending (the request is in flight), fulfilled (the response arrived), or rejected (a network failure occurred before any response). Cover .then(response => ...) for handling fulfillment: the response object has a status property and a body. Cover the critical two-step: first check response.ok (true for 2xx status codes), then call response.json() which also returns a Promise because it reads the response stream asynchronously. Cover .catch(error => ...) for handling rejection. Cover Promise.all([p1, p2]) for running multiple fetches in parallel and waiting for all to complete.
Learning Outcome: Student can explain what an API is, describe the client-server request-response cycle, identify common HTTP status codes, write a fetch call using .then().catch(), check response.ok before parsing, and use Promise.all for parallel requests.
Activity: Fetch Basics: fetch data from a public API (JSONPlaceholder /posts and /users) using .then().catch(). Log the raw response object, its status, and the parsed JSON. Handle a 404 by requesting a non-existent resource and logging the status code. Fetch posts and users simultaneously using Promise.all and log the combined results. Write a reusable fetchJSON(url) function that returns a Promise resolving to parsed data or rejecting with a descriptive error.
Project: Chatbot API Integration: update chatbot-logic.js to add a fetchBotResponse(userMessage) function that sends the user's message to a public echo or chat API using fetch. Handle the two-step correctly: check response.ok before calling response.json(). Return the response text. This function will be called from the DOM layer in the next article.

Description: Cover async/await as syntactic sugar over .then().catch() Promises — it does not change how Promises work, it changes how the code reads. Marking a function with async makes it return a Promise automatically. The await keyword inside an async function pauses execution until the awaited Promise settles, then evaluates to the resolved value. Compare the same fetch written with .then().catch() and with async/await side by side to show they produce identical behavior with different readability. Cover that await can only appear directly inside an async function — using it in a regular function throws a SyntaxError. Cover top-level await in ES modules as a brief mention. Cover async arrow functions. Cover the common mistake of forgetting await and receiving a Promise object instead of the resolved value. Cover sequential vs parallel execution with async/await: awaiting inside a loop makes each request wait for the previous (sequential), while creating all Promises first and awaiting Promise.all is parallel. Cover the difference between an async function throwing an error and returning a rejected Promise — they are equivalent, but only try/catch (not .catch()) handles a thrown error inside async.
Learning Outcome: Student can convert any .then().catch() chain to equivalent async/await code, explain why await can only appear in an async function, write parallel fetches using Promise.all with async/await, and identify the sequential-vs-parallel pitfall inside loops.
Activity: async/await Conversion: rewrite the fetchJSON function from the previous article using async/await. Rewrite the Promise.all example using async/await. Write an async function fetchMultipleSequential(urls) that fetches each URL one after the other and returns all results in order. Write fetchMultipleParallel(urls) that fetches all simultaneously. Time both with a large array of URLs using console.time and compare.
Project: Chatbot API Integration: rewrite fetchBotResponse(userMessage) from the previous article using async/await. The function is now cleaner and directly returns the awaited parsed response. Call it from the send message handler using await inside an async function. Confirm the behavior is identical to the .then() version by testing with the same input.

Topic: Error Handling Loading States and UI Feedback
Description: Cover the three states every data-driven UI must handle explicitly: loading (the request is in flight and the user is waiting), success (data arrived and is ready to display), and error (something went wrong). A UI that does not handle all three leaves users staring at a blank screen. Cover try/catch around await: when the awaited Promise rejects (network failure, timeout) the error is caught by catch. Cover non-200 responses: fetch does not reject for 4xx or 5xx status codes — the Promise fulfills with a response object that has response.ok set to false. Check response.ok inside the try block and throw a custom Error if it is false, so the catch block handles both network errors and bad status codes uniformly. Cover the finally block: runs after try or catch regardless of outcome — the correct place to hide a loading spinner or re-enable a submit button. Cover empty state: when the request succeeds but the data array is empty, the UI needs to display a helpful message rather than a blank list. Cover the four-state model: loading, success with data, success with empty data, error. Cover AbortController for canceling a fetch in flight when the component unmounts or the user navigates away.
Learning Outcome: Student can implement all three UI states for an async operation, distinguish network errors from non-200 HTTP responses, handle both with a single catch block by throwing inside the try block, use finally for cleanup, and render an empty state separately from an error state.
Activity: Four-State UI: build a news feed component that fetches articles from a public API. Implement all four states: show a loading skeleton while fetching, show the article list when data arrives, show an empty-state message if the response is an empty array, and show an error message with a retry button if the fetch fails. The retry button re-runs the fetch. Use AbortController to cancel the in-flight request if the user clicks a different category before the first request completes.
Project: Chatbot API Integration: update the send message handler in the chatbot to manage all four states. When the user sends a message: immediately show a typing indicator by setting isLoading true and calling renderChat, await fetchBotResponse, on success push the bot response to state.messages, on error set state.error and show an error bubble with a retry option, in finally set isLoading false and re-enable the input. The chatbot now has a fully wired DOM interface connected to a real API — completing the arc from the pure JS logic built in JS_Basics to a working interactive product.
